content.text: Geometric constructions involve drawing geometric figures with precision using minimal tools. The most basic tools are an unmarked ruler (straightedge) and a compass. These tools allow us to create exact geometric shapes without measuring or calculating specific lengths, making constructions geometrically rigorous and mathematically pure.

content.title: Eye Construction
procedure: Draw a line segment XY as a supporting line.
Find points A and B such that AX = AY = BX = BY (using arcs from X and Y).
Join A and B with a line.
The line AB is perpendicular to XY and bisects it at point O.
content.observation: The resulting shape forms a symmetric eye with upper and lower arcs of equal radius.
content.conclusion: Symmetric figures can be constructed using the property that points equidistant from two endpoints lie on the perpendicular bisector.

content.title: Importance of Supporting Lines
content.text: Supporting lines and arcs guide construction but are not part of the final figure. They create the framework upon which actual figures are drawn. Understanding how to work with these supporting elements is critical to all geometric constructions.

A line segment can be divided into two equal parts in many ways, but there is a unique straight line that divides it into two identical parts and is perpendicular to it. This line is called the perpendicular bisector. The perpendicular bisector has a special property: any point on it is equidistant from the two endpoints of the original segment.

content.title: Constructing Perpendicular Bisector
procedure: Mark two points A and B above line XY, such that AX = AY = BX = BY.
Mark two points A' and B' below line XY, such that A'X = A'Y = B'X = B'Y.
Join AB' (or AA' or BB'). This is the perpendicular bisector.
Verify: The bisector passes through the midpoint of XY and forms a 90° angle with XY.
content.observation: The constructed line passes through the midpoint of XY and is perpendicular to it.
content.conclusion: By constructing congruent triangles (via equal distances), we prove that this line is the unique perpendicular bisector.

content.title: Key Property: Equidistant Points
content.text: Any point P on the perpendicular bisector of segment XY must satisfy: PX = PY. Conversely, any point equidistant from X and Y must lie on the perpendicular bisector. This is the fundamental principle behind the construction.

content.title: Why This Method Works (Congruence Proof)
items: Consider triangles AOX and AOY where O is the intersection of AB and XY.
We have: AX = AY (given), AO is common, and ∠XAO = ∠YAO (from congruent triangles ABX and ABY).
By SAS congruence: ΔAOX ≅ ΔAOY.
Therefore: OX = OY (O is the midpoint) and ∠AOX = ∠AOY = 90°.

content.text: A 90° angle (right angle) can be constructed at any point on a line using the perpendicular bisector method. This is achieved by creating a point equidistant from both sides of a chosen point, ensuring the perpendicular bisector is perpendicular to the original line.

content.title: Constructing 90° Angle at a Point
procedure: Mark a point O on the given line.
Using compass, mark points X and Y equidistant from O on the line, so O is the midpoint of XY.
Construct the perpendicular bisector of XY.
This perpendicular bisector makes a 90° angle with the original line at O.
content.observation: The constructed line is perpendicular to the given line at point O.
content.conclusion: By leveraging the perpendicular bisector property, we can construct right angles anywhere on a line.

content.title: Efficiency in Right Angle Construction
content.text: When constructing a 90° angle at a point already on a line, we only need one pair of intersecting arcs (not two pairs as in the general perpendicular bisector construction). This is because the point already lies on the perpendicular bisector.

content.title: Historical Connection: Sulba-Sutras
items: Ancient Indian texts (Sulba-Sutras) recorded methods for constructing perpendiculars and right angles.
These methods were used in constructing fire altars for rituals during the Vedic period.
Traditional construction used ropes instead of compasses, with the same geometric principles.

content.text: Any angle can be divided into two equal parts (bisected) using a compass and unmarked ruler. Similarly, an angle can be copied exactly at a different location. Both these constructions rely on creating congruent triangles, where corresponding angles are proven equal through congruence.

content.title: Bisecting an Angle
procedure: Mark points A and B on the two rays of angle XOY, such that OA = OB.
Draw arcs from A and B with equal radius.
Mark the intersection point as C.
Draw ray OC. This bisects angle XOY.
content.observation: The ray OC divides angle XOY into two equal angles.
content.conclusion: By SSS congruence (ΔOBC ≅ ΔOAC), we have ∠BOC = ∠AOC.

content.title: Copying an Angle
procedure: Draw an arc from the vertex of the original angle, marking points on both rays.
Draw the same arc from the new vertex position.
Measure the distance between the two points on the original angle.
Transfer this distance to the new arc, creating two points.
Connect the new vertex to this second point.
content.observation: The angle constructed at the new location equals the original angle.
content.conclusion: By SSS congruence of the triangles formed, ∠A = ∠X.

content.title: Applications: Constructing Special Angles
content.text: Using bisection repeatedly, we can construct various angles: bisect a 90° angle to get 45°, bisect again to get 22.5°, etc. Similarly, constructing a 60° angle (equilateral triangle) and bisecting gives 30°, and bisecting again gives 15°.

content.title: Constructible Angles (Examples)
items: 90°: Constructed using perpendicular bisector method
60°: Constructed using equilateral triangle
45°: Bisect a 90° angle
30°: Bisect a 60° angle
15°: Bisect a 30° angle
120°: Combine 60° angles

topics.topic_name: Parallel Lines Using Compass and Ruler

content.text: Parallel lines can be constructed using the principle of corresponding angles. When two lines are cut by a transversal and create equal corresponding angles, the lines are parallel. This principle allows us to construct a line parallel to a given line using only a compass and unmarked ruler.

content.title: Constructing Parallel Lines
procedure: Take a line m to which a parallel is needed.
Draw transversal l intersecting m at point A.
Choose point B on line l above A.
Copy the angle at A onto point B using compass and ruler.
The line through B with this copied angle is parallel to m.
content.observation: The constructed line through B is parallel to the original line m.
content.conclusion: Equal corresponding angles guarantee parallel lines (Converse of Corresponding Angles Theorem).

content.title: Connection to Angle Copying
content.text: The construction of parallel lines directly applies the angle-copying technique. By ensuring the corresponding angles are equal, we guarantee the lines will never meet and are therefore parallel.

content.title: Key Geometric Principle
items: If a transversal cuts two lines and creates equal corresponding angles, the lines are parallel.
This principle can be reversed: equal corresponding angles can be created with compass and ruler.
By creating equal angles at different points on different lines, we ensure they are parallel.

content.text: Beautiful arch designs seen in architecture can be constructed using geometric principles. Regular polygons, especially regular hexagons, have special properties that make them constructible with compass and ruler. The hexagon is particularly special because it can be decomposed into equilateral triangles.

content.title: Constructing a Trefoil Arch
procedure: Draw support lines with equal segments: AB = CD.
Construct equal angles at A and D: ∠BAD = ∠CDA.
From A and D, draw arcs with chosen radius.
These arcs form the trefoil (three-lobed) arch shape.
content.observation: The arch has three symmetric lobes and is aesthetically pleasing.
content.conclusion: Symmetric support with equal angles and arcs creates balanced arch designs.

content.title: Constructing a Regular Hexagon
procedure: Draw a line segment as one side of length 5 cm.
Construct a 60° angle at one endpoint of the segment.
Mark a point at distance 5 cm along the new ray (same side length).
Repeat: construct 60° angles and mark 5 cm segments six times.
The closing point will be the starting point, forming a regular hexagon.
content.observation: All sides are equal (5 cm) and all interior angles are 120°.
content.conclusion: Each interior angle of a regular hexagon = (6-2)×180°/6 = 120°.

content.title: 60° Angle and Equilateral Triangle Connection
content.text: A 60° angle is constructed by making an equilateral triangle. In an equilateral triangle, all angles are 60°. Six such 60° angles fit perfectly around a point (6 × 60° = 360°), allowing six equilateral triangles to form a regular hexagon.

content.title: Regular Polygons and Angles
items: Triangle: Interior angle = 60°, All equilateral triangles can tile a plane
Square: Interior angle = 90°, Squares can tile a plane
Regular Pentagon: Interior angle = 108°, Cannot tile a plane alone
Regular Hexagon: Interior angle = 120°, Six triangles form one hexagon, Hexagons can tile a plane

content.text: Tiling (or tessellation) is the process of covering a region with a set of shapes such that there are no gaps between pieces and no overlapping. The shapes fit together perfectly to completely cover the region. Tangrams are an ancient puzzle consisting of 7 pieces that can be rearranged to form various figures.

content.definition: Covering a plane or region using a set of shapes without gaps (holes) or overlaps (pieces stacked on each other).

content.title: Tangram Puzzle
procedure: A square is divided into 7 pieces: 2 large triangles, 1 medium triangle, 2 small triangles, 1 square, 1 parallelogram.
These 7 pieces can be rearranged to form many different shapes (arrow, cat, boat, person, etc.)
All arrangements cover exactly the same area as the original square.
content.observation: Different shapes can be formed, but the total area remains constant.
content.conclusion: Rearranging pieces without gaps or overlaps is the essence of tiling.

content.title: What Makes a Good Tiling?
content.text: A good tiling must satisfy: (1) Complete coverage with no gaps, (2) No overlaps or pieces stacked on each other, (3) Efficient use of space, (4) Often aesthetic appeal in design.

A fundamental question in tiling is: given a region with a certain number of unit squares, can it be tiled using 2×1 tiles? This seemingly simple question reveals deep mathematical principles. The answer depends on the dimensions of the region and which unit squares are removed.

content.title: Tiling a 4×6 Grid
procedure: A 4×6 grid has 24 unit squares (4 rows, 6 columns).
Each 2×1 tile covers exactly 2 unit squares.
Since 24 = 12 × 2, we need 12 tiles.
Tiles can be placed vertically (occupying 2 rows in 1 column) or horizontally (occupying 1 row in 2 columns).
content.observation: One simple strategy: cover each column with 2 vertical tiles (4 squares per column ÷ 2 = 2 tiles per column).
content.conclusion: A 4×6 grid (even × even) can always be tiled with 2×1 tiles.

content.title: Non-tileable Regions and Parity Argument
procedure: Consider a 5×7 grid: 35 unit squares total.
Each 2×1 tile covers 2 squares.
35 ÷ 2 = 17.5 (not a whole number).
Therefore, 35 unit squares cannot be covered by 2×1 tiles.
content.observation: A region with an odd number of squares can never be tiled by 2×1 tiles.
content.conclusion: Parity (even/odd count) is a necessary condition for tileability.

content.title: Tileability Conditions for m×n Grids
content.text: An m×n grid is tileable with 2×1 tiles if: (1) Both m and n are even, OR (2) At least one of m or n is even. An m×n grid is NOT tileable if both m and n are odd (odd total squares).

content.title: Tileable Grid Examples
items: 4×6 grid (even × even): Tileable. Strategy: Use vertical tiles in each column.
4×7 grid (even × odd): Tileable. Odd columns (7) × even rows (4) = even total squares (28).
5×6 grid (odd × even): Tileable. Odd rows (5) × even columns (6) = even total squares (30).
5×7 grid (odd × odd): NOT tileable. Odd × odd = odd total squares (35).

notes.section_title: Advanced Tiling: Coloring Strategy and Plane Tiling

topics.topic_name: Coloring Strategy and Tiling the Entire Plane

content.text: When parity alone is insufficient to determine tileability, a clever coloring strategy using black and white squares helps determine whether a region can be tiled. Additionally, certain shapes can tile the entire infinite plane. This raises interesting questions about which regular polygons and shapes have this property.

content.title: Black-and-White Coloring for Tileability
procedure: Color the grid like a checkerboard: alternating black and white squares.
Each 2×1 tile covers exactly one black and one white square.
If the region has unequal black and white squares, it cannot be tiled with 2×1 tiles.
Example: A 5×3 grid with one corner square removed might have 8 white and 6 black squares.
content.observation: The region with 8 white and 6 black squares cannot be tiled (8 ≠ 6).
content.conclusion: Equal numbers of black and white squares are necessary (and often sufficient) for tiling with 2×1 tiles.

content.title: Why Coloring Works
content.text: A 2×1 tile always covers one black and one white square (properties of the checkerboard). Therefore, any valid tiling must use equal numbers of black and white tiles, which is only possible if the region has equal black and white squares. This provides a rigorous proof of non-tileability.

content.title: Tiling the Entire Plane
procedure: Regular squares tile the plane (4 squares meet at each vertex).
Equilateral triangles tile the plane (6 triangles meet at each vertex).
Regular hexagons tile the plane (3 hexagons meet at each vertex).
Regular pentagons do NOT tile the plane alone (interior angle = 108°, no whole number of pentagons fit at a vertex).
content.observation: Only specific regular polygons can tile the plane without gaps.
content.conclusion: For regular polygons to tile: interior angle must divide 360° evenly. Examples: 60° (triangle), 90° (square), 120° (hexagon).

content.title: Plane-Tiling Possibilities
items: Single regular polygon: Triangles, squares, hexagons only
Multiple regular polygons: Various combinations exist (e.g., octagons with squares)
Non-regular polygons: Any parallelogram can tile the plane
Artistic tilings: Dutch artist M.C. Escher created intricate tilings with animal shapes and artistic designs

content.title: Tiling in Nature and Architecture
content.text: Hexagonal tilings appear in honeycomb structures made by bees and wasps. These hexagonal cells efficiently store food and protect eggs/larvae with minimal material. The mathematical optimization nature achieves is still studied by scientists. Tilings are also prominent in building designs and decorative patterns.

glossary.definition: Dividing a line segment or angle into two congruent (identical) parts.
A line that bisects a given line segment and is perpendicular (at 90°) to it. Any point on the perpendicular bisector is equidistant from the endpoints of the segment.
The process of drawing geometric figures precisely using only basic tools like an unmarked ruler and compass, without measuring.
A tool with two arms (one with a pointed tip, one with a pencil) used to draw circles and arcs of specific radius in geometric constructions.
A straightedge without markings or measurements, used only to draw straight lines in geometric constructions.
A ray that divides an angle into two congruent angles of equal measure.
Angles in the same relative position at two different intersections when a transversal crosses two lines. Equal corresponding angles indicate the lines are parallel.
A line that intersects two or more other lines at different points.
Two or more lines in the same plane that never intersect and maintain constant distance from each other.
A polygon with all sides of equal length and all angles of equal measure.
A triangle with all three sides equal in length and all three angles equal to 60°.
A six-sided regular polygon with all sides equal and all interior angles equal to 120°. It can be divided into six equilateral triangles.
Covering a plane or region with shapes (tiles) such that there are no gaps and no overlaps.
One of the individual shapes used in a tiling pattern to cover a region or plane.
A rectangular tile with dimensions 2 units by 1 unit, used to cover grids of unit squares in tiling problems.
An ancient Chinese puzzle consisting of 7 geometric pieces (2 large triangles, 1 medium triangle, 2 small triangles, 1 square, 1 parallelogram) that can be arranged to form various shapes.
A pattern where adjacent squares (in a grid) alternate between two colors (usually black and white), used to analyze tiling problems.
The property of geometric figures being identical in shape and size. Congruent figures can be mapped onto each other through transformations.
The angle inside a polygon at each vertex. For a regular n-gon, each interior angle measures (n-2)×180°/n degrees.

summary_points: Geometric constructions using unmarked ruler and compass are based on the principle that any point equidistant from two given points lies on the perpendicular bisector of the line segment joining them.
The perpendicular bisector of a line segment can be constructed by drawing two pairs of intersecting arcs (one pair above and one pair below) from the endpoints of the segment.
A right angle (90°) can be constructed at any point on a line by finding the perpendicular bisector of a segment for which that point is the midpoint.
Any angle can be bisected using compass and ruler by constructing congruent triangles (SSS congruence), and any angle can be copied at a different location using the same principle.
Parallel lines can be constructed using the property of corresponding angles: if equal corresponding angles are created using a transversal, the lines are guaranteed to be parallel.
A 60° angle is constructed using an equilateral triangle, and other angles like 30°, 45°, 120° can be constructed by bisecting or combining these basic angles.

A regular hexagon can be constructed as a combination of six equilateral triangles, with each interior angle measuring 120°. The vertices lie on a circle, and opposite vertices connect through the center.
Tiling is the process of covering a region with shapes (tiles) without gaps or overlaps. For an m×n grid to be tileable with 2×1 tiles, the total number of unit squares must be even.
The coloring strategy (checkerboard pattern) provides a powerful tool to determine tileability: if a region has unequal numbers of black and white squares, it cannot be tiled with 2×1 tiles.

Only three regular polygons can tile the entire plane individually: equilateral triangles (interior angle 60°), squares (interior angle 90°), and regular hexagons (interior angle 120°). Pentagon and other regular polygons cannot tile alone.
